sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 57-year-old male, presents with a 2-week history of shortness of breath, which worsens with exertion.
He also reports coughing up blood, approximately 3-4 cups per day, for the past 3 weeks.
He has a history of diabetes, high blood pressure, and asthma.
He has been experiencing difficulty controlling his blood sugar, with readings often over 200 after meals and around 150 in the morning.
His blood pressure has been running low at times, and he has been taking norvasc as prescribed.
He denies fever, bruising, or bleeding from his teeth or gums.
Past Medical History: The patient has a history of diabetes, high blood pressure, and asthma.
He is currently taking metformin 500mg twice daily for his diabetes.
Review of Systems: The patient reports shortness of breath, coughing up blood, and difficulty controlling his blood sugar.
Current Medications: Metformin 500mg twice daily for diabetes, Norvasc (dose and frequency not specified) for high blood pressure.
Objective:
Vital Signs: Blood pressure 120/70, pulse rate not specified, oxygen saturation 94%, temperature not specified.
Physical Exam: The patient has no jugular venous distension, no carotid bruits, and no swelling in the neck.
The lung exam reveals rails and rhonchi on the right side, diminished breath sounds on the right side, and a 2/6 systolic ejection murmur.
The heart exam is otherwise regular rate and rhythm.
The belly exam is soft and non-tender.
The extremity exam reveals 1+ nonpitting edema on both lower extremities, with no calf tenderness or Homan's sign.
Test Results: Not mentioned.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient is experiencing a respiratory issue, likely pneumonia, with coughing up blood and shortness of breath.
Problem List: Pneumonia (confirmed); Diabetes (uncontrolled); Hypertension (uncontrolled).
Plan:
I prescribed metformin 750mg twice daily to increase the dose to control his diabetes.
I ordered a hemoglobin A1c to check his blood sugar control.
I referred the patient to the emergency department for a chest x-ray, CT scan of the chest, and blood work to evaluate the cause of his shortness of breath and coughing up blood.
I instructed the patient to hold off on taking norvasc until the episode of shortness of breath is resolved.
Follow-up: I will have the patient return in 3 weeks to discuss his diabetes treatment plan and reassess his blood pressure.
